title: Deterministic Single-Photon Emitter Arrays in Hexagonal Boron Nitride by Carbon-Assisted Focused Ion Beam Engineering
authors: Mangababu Akkanaboina, Rohit Kumar, Brijesh Kumar, Hrushikesh Gawali, Parul Sharma, Ikshvaku Shyam, Anshuman Kumar
abstract: The realization of on-chip photonic circuits requires scalable and deterministic single-photon emitters (SPEs) at room temperature, which remain a challenge in van der Waals materials. In this work, we report a novel three-step fabrication process for the generation of spatially controlled SPE arrays in hexagonal boron nitride (hBN). The process comprises site-selective gallium (Ga) focused ion beam milling, nanoscale conformal carbon deposition over the patterned regions, and subsequent thermal annealing. The synergistic combination of these steps resulted in a site-correlated emitter yield of ($\sim 89\%$) across 100 fabrication sites. Second-order autocorrelation measurements revealed pronounced three-level emitter dynamics where the best emitters exhibited high purity ($g^{(2)}(0)=0.15 \pm 0.09$).To the best of our knowledge, this is the first lithography-free, direct-write approach combining Ga-ion milling, selective carbon engineering, and thermal annealing to deterministically generate \hBN{} \SPE{}s. The reproducibility of the method is validated across multiple independently fabricated samples. These results establish a scalable, lithography-free pathway toward on-demand SPE arrays relevant to integrated quantum photonics.
summary: The authors report a breakthrough lithography-free technique using focused ion beam milling and carbon deposition to deterministically create arrays of single-photon emitters in hBN. By achieving high site yield and confirming single-photon purity via $g^{(2)}$ measurements, this work establishes a scalable platform crucial for integrated quantum optical circuits.
main_problem: The challenge is creating scalable, deterministic single-photon emitters (SPEs) at room temperature within van der Waals materials for on-chip quantum photonic circuits.
main_result: A novel, lithography-free three-step process was developed to deterministically generate SPE arrays in hBN with high site-correlated yields ($\sim 89\%$) and confirmed single-photon purity ($g^{(2)}(0) = 0.15 \pm 0.09$).
method: The method involves a sequential fabrication process: Gallium focused ion beam milling, followed by conformal carbon deposition, and final thermal annealing.
model_or_system: The physical platform is hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) hosting defect-related SPEs. The emission dynamics are analyzed using a three-level emitter model to fit the second-order autocorrelation function $g^{(2)}(	au)$.
key_observables: Second-order autocorrelation function ($g^{(2)}(0)$), Zero-Phonon-Line (ZPL) wavelength ($\sim 581 	ext{ nm}$), and site-correlated emitter yield.
important_parameters: Annealing temperature ($850^\circ	ext{C}$), milling depth ($\sim 40 	ext{ nm}$), and the single-photon purity threshold ($g^{(2)}(0) < 0.5$).
main_assumptions: The precise microscopic origin of the emitting centers is not fully resolved, and defect migration during annealing is assumed to follow classical diffusion theory.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the three-step fabrication process (milling $ightarrow$ carbon deposition $ightarrow$ annealing), PL maps showing emission spots, and $g^{(2)}(	au)$ measurements confirming single-photon antibunching for representative emitters.
paper_structure: The paper details the necessity of deterministic SPEs, presents the novel three-step fabrication protocol, characterizes the resulting arrays using PL mapping and quantum correlation measurements ($g^{(2)}$), and validates the method's reproducibility.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses a key hardware bottleneck for integrated quantum photonics by providing a scalable, direct-write pathway to high-quality SPE sources in 2D materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a) Schematic representation of sample preparation where the first step was milling the mechanically exfoliated hBN flake of thickness 120 nm using Ga+-ion based FIB, followed by selective carbon deposition at the milled spot and subsequent annealing at 850 ◦C in an Ar environment. b) Optical microscope image of the processed hBN flake. c) AFM topography of the FIB-milled region marked by yellow rectangle in (b). d) Corresponding AFM height profile extracted across the milled spots, revealing a milling depth of ∼40 nm.

FIG. 2. (a) Optical image of the milled spots where PL map is performed, (b) its corresponding PL map generated at a wavelength of 581 nm depicting luminescent array of milled spots. Statistical analysis of all 10 × 10 milled spots is shown in (c), (d), and (e). The scatter plot in (d) displays ZPL wavelengths of 89 emitters found with in 10×10 milled spots on Y-axis and their corresponding FWHM on X-axis, (C) displays the histogram of counts (number of emitters) vs FWHM, and (e) histogram of number of emitters vs wavelength

FIG. 3. Confirmation of quantum emitters. (a) PL spectra of four different emitters identified in the mapped region. (b-e) Second-order autocorrelation measurements, g(2)(τ), of the corresponding emitters shown in (a), confirming their single-photon emission characteristics.

FIG. 4. Optical stability and performance metrics of emitters: (a) Temporal stability measurement under continuous excitation, recorded over 5 min with a time bin of 0.5 s. (b) Second-order correlation g(2)(0) as a function of excitation power ranging from 0.5 to 4 mW, confirming single-photon emission behavior. (c) Second-order correlation g(2)(0) and characteristic antibunching time as a function of power. (d) Power-dependent photoluminescence saturation curve of the emitter.
